moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with a quiet, flexible move that controls the central square e5 and prepares to develop our kingside. The Nimzo-Larsen Attack (1.b3 followed by Bb2) is an unorthodox opening that forgoes the traditional central battle—instead, we'll control the center from afar with our fianchettoed bishop on the long diagonal. This move keeps all our options open while developing with tempo.

Black stakes an immediate claim in the center, which is natural and strong. By playing d5, Black challenges our space and prevents us from establishing a pawn on e4 without thought. This move also opens lines for Black's light-squared bishop and queen.

Now we commit to the Nimzo-Larsen setup: we'll fianchetto our bishop to control the long diagonal (a1-h8) and support our eventual central play. Playing b3 also prepares Bb2, which will exert long-range pressure toward e5 and g7—a key square in our strategy. This slower approach suits positions where we want to maintain flexibility and gradually build pressure rather than meet Black head-on.

Black develops naturally and defends the e4 square, anticipating our plan. The knight on f6 also controls important squares like e4 and d5, making it harder for us to advance our pawns. Black is playing logically, creating a solid center and preparing further development.

We complete our fianchetto setup, placing the bishop on the long diagonal where it eyes the center and Black's kingside. The bishop on b2 is perfectly placed to support a future e4 advance and controls the critical d4 square. This is the thematic move of the Nimzo-Larsen Attack—we're not playing for immediate tactics, but rather for long-term pressure and control.

Black challenges our center space and prevents us from simply playing e4 unopposed. By advancing c5, Black also gains space on the queenside and prepares to support the d5 pawn or open lines for counterplay. This is an active response that commits Black to a dynamic middlegame structure.

Now we strike at the center! With c5 played, we can advance e4 with good reason: we're attacking the d5 pawn, and our Bb2 supports this advance by controlling the important central square d4. The move exd4 would leave us with a strong pawn on d4 and active piece play. This gambit (if Black takes) offers us open lines and piece activity as compensation—typical of the Norfolk Gambit's fighting spirit.
